walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Sector Tiles
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Gaia Project, a game of galactic colonization. Let's start by building the galaxy. Find the ten double-sided sector tiles. Each tile shows a mix of planet types and empty space hexes. Arrange them according to one of the suggested layouts in the rulebook, or create your own configuration.

walkthrough.title: Place the Research Board
walkthrough.text: Now set the research board next to the galaxy map where everyone can see it. This board shows six research tracks running from level zero to level five. The six tracks are Terraforming, Navigation, Artificial Intelligence, Gaiaforming, Economy, and Science.

walkthrough.title: Add Technology Tiles to Research Board
walkthrough.text: Place the standard technology tiles face up on the research board near the level three and four positions of each track. Then place one advanced technology tile face up on the level five position of each track. These are powerful rewards for the first player to reach the top of each track.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Round Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the round scoring tiles and randomly place one face up on each of the six round spaces. These tiles determine what actions earn bonus victory points each round. Take a moment to read all six so everyone knows what is coming.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out Round Boosters
walkthrough.text: Take a number of round boosters equal to the player count plus three and place them face up near the board. For example, in a three-player game you would lay out six boosters. Return any extras to the box without looking at them.

walkthrough.title: Select Final Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Randomly draw two final scoring tiles and place them face up near the research board. These tiles determine which categories award bonus victory points at the end of the game. They can be worth up to eighteen points each, so pay close attention to what they reward.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all resource tokens, federation tokens, and action tokens in a shared supply area within reach of all players. Organize the federation tokens by type so everyone can see what is available. The supply also holds any unclaimed Gaiaformer tokens.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Each player selects one of the fourteen factions. The factions are organized into seven pairs, each tied to a native planet type. For your first game, we recommend Terrans, Hadsch Hallas, or Geodens since they have straightforward abilities. Two factions that share the same planet type cannot both be in the same game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Faction Board
walkthrough.text: Take your faction board and populate it with all of your wooden structures. Place your Mines, Trading Stations, Research Labs, Planetary Institute, and Academies on the corresponding tracks. As you build structures onto the map during the game, you will uncover higher income values underneath.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Power Bowls
walkthrough.text: Set up your power tokens in the three bowl areas on your faction board as indicated by your faction sheet. Most factions start with some tokens in Bowl one and some in Bowl two. These tokens cycle through the bowls as you gain and spend power throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Take your starting Ore, Credits, Knowledge, and Q.I.C. tokens as shown on your faction board. Each faction begins with different amounts, so read your board carefully. Also place your research markers at the starting positions on the six research tracks.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Mines
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player places one Mine on a planet of their native type on the galaxy map. Then in reverse order again, each player places a second Mine. Placement is critical because these starting positions determine your expansion options and potential federation clusters for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Select Round Boosters
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each player selects one of the available round boosters for Round one. Your booster provides income at the start of each round and a pass bonus when you pass at the end of a round. Look at what the round scoring tiles reward in Rounds one and two to help guide your choice.

walkthrough.title: Verify and Begin
walkthrough.text: Let's do a final check. Everyone should have a faction board with structures covering income tracks, power tokens in the correct bowls, starting resources, two Mines on the map, and a round booster. The research board, round scoring tiles, final scoring tiles, and technology tiles should all be visible. Ready to colonize the galaxy? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Gaia Project is played over six rounds. In each round, players take turns performing one action at a time until everyone has passed. The goal is to colonize planets, build structures, advance on research tracks, and form federations to score the most victory points.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round has four phases. First is the Income Phase where everyone collects resources simultaneously. Second is the Gaia Phase where Gaiaforming projects resolve. Third is the Action Phase where players alternate taking one action each. Fourth is the Clean Up Phase where round scoring is resolved.

walkthrough.title: Income Phase
walkthrough.text: During income, you collect resources based on the uncovered tracks on your faction board. Mines generate Ore, Trading Stations generate Credits, Research Labs generate Knowledge, and your Planetary Institute and Academies generate Power and other resources. Your round booster may also provide additional income.

walkthrough.title: Gaia Phase
walkthrough.text: If you committed power tokens to the Gaia area on a previous turn for a Gaiaforming project, those tokens now move back to Bowl one. The Transdimensional planet where you placed a Gaiaformer becomes a Gaia planet, ready for you to build on. If you have no active Gaiaforming projects, simply skip this phase.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of the game. Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player takes exactly one action on their turn. You keep going around the table until everyone has passed. There are seven possible actions to choose from on each turn.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build a Mine
walkthrough.text: To colonize a new planet, it must be within your building range, which starts at one and increases with Navigation research. If the planet matches your native type, simply pay the Ore and Credit cost to place a Mine. If the planet is a different type, you must terraform it first by paying extra Ore based on the number of steps on the terraforming wheel.

walkthrough.title: Terraforming Details
walkthrough.text: Planet types are arranged on a transformation wheel. Count the minimum steps between the target planet and your native type. Each step costs Ore, and the amount depends on your Terraforming research level. At level zero each step costs three Ore, but advancing your research reduces this significantly. At level five you can terraform any planet in a single step.

walkthrough.title: Action: Upgrade a Structure
walkthrough.text: You can upgrade structures along specific paths. A Mine upgrades to a Trading Station. A Trading Station can become either a Research Lab or a Planetary Institute. A Research Lab upgrades to an Academy. Each upgrade costs Ore and Credits, and the upgrade removes the old structure from the map, replacing it with the new one.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Benefits
walkthrough.text: Each upgrade uncovers income tracks on your faction board and provides unique benefits. Trading Stations unlock Credit income and cost less if adjacent to an opponent. Research Labs grant Knowledge income plus a technology tile. Your Planetary Institute unlocks your faction's special ability. Academies grant an advanced technology tile.

walkthrough.title: Action: Advance on a Research Track
walkthrough.text: Spend Knowledge to move one step up on any of the six research tracks. Each track provides escalating passive bonuses as you advance. Reaching level three lets you claim a standard technology tile, and reaching level five grants an advanced technology tile plus a large one-time bonus.

walkthrough.title: The Six Research Tracks
walkthrough.text: Let's quickly cover what each track does. Terraforming reduces the Ore cost per step. Navigation increases your building range. Artificial Intelligence grants Q.I.C. income. Gaiaforming reduces the power cost to start Gaiaforming projects. Economy provides Credit and Ore income. Science provides Knowledge income.

walkthrough.title: Action: Power and Q.I.C. Actions
walkthrough.text: You can spend power tokens from Bowl three to take powerful one-time actions shown on the research board. These include gaining resources, terraforming a planet, or placing a Mine. You can also spend Q.I.C. tokens for even stronger actions like gaining a free technology tile or placing a Mine anywhere on the map ignoring range.

walkthrough.title: Action: Special Actions
walkthrough.text: Some technology tiles, round boosters, and faction abilities provide special actions marked with a unique icon. Each special action can only be used once per round. After using it, flip or mark the tile to show it has been spent. These reset at the start of the next round.

walkthrough.title: Action: Form a Federation
walkthrough.text: When you have a connected group of structures with a combined power value of at least seven, you can form a federation. Claim a federation token from the supply, which provides victory points and resources. This is one of the most important scoring opportunities in the game.

walkthrough.title: Federation Details
walkthrough.text: Structures contribute different power values: a Mine is worth one, a Trading Station is worth two, and a Planetary Institute is worth four. You can place power tokens on empty space hexes as satellites to connect structures that are not directly adjacent. Once structures are part of a federation, they cannot be counted toward a new one.

walkthrough.title: Action: Pass
walkthrough.text: When you pass, you return your current round booster and select a new one for the next round. Before returning it, you collect the pass bonus printed on your booster, which typically awards victory points based on your board state. You take no more actions for the rest of the round.

walkthrough.title: The Power Cycle
walkthrough.text: Power tokens move through three bowls plus a Gaia area. When you gain power, tokens move from Bowl one to Bowl two, then from Bowl two to Bowl three. Only tokens in Bowl three can be spent, and spent tokens return to Bowl one. You may also burn tokens from Bowl two, removing them permanently, to fast-track power to Bowl three.

walkthrough.title: Leeching Power
walkthrough.text: When an opponent builds or upgrades a structure within range two of one of your structures, you may leech power. You gain power equal to the opponent's new structure's power value. However, leeching costs one victory point for each power gained beyond the first. You can always choose to leech less or decline entirely.

walkthrough.title: Gaiaforming
walkthrough.text: Transdimensional planets cannot be directly terraformed. Instead, place a Gaiaformer on the planet and commit power tokens to the Gaia area. During the next Gaia Phase, the planet becomes a Gaia planet. You can then build a Mine on it, treating it as just one terraforming step from your native type.

walkthrough.title: Technology Tiles
walkthrough.text: Standard technology tiles provide ongoing income, one-time bonuses, or special actions. You gain them by reaching level three or four on a research track or by upgrading to a Research Lab. Advanced technology tiles are much more powerful and are claimed by reaching level five on a track or upgrading to an Academy. Each advanced tile is unique and first-come-first-served.

walkthrough.title: Clean Up Phase
walkthrough.text: After everyone has passed, resolve the current round's scoring tile. Players earn victory points for specific actions they took during the round, such as building Mines or upgrading structures. Then advance the round marker and begin the next round with a fresh Income Phase.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After Round six, final scoring occurs. You score victory points from your research track positions, where each level provides end-game points. You also score the two final scoring tiles, which award eighteen, twelve, six, or zero points for first through fourth place in their respective categories.

walkthrough.title: Resource Conversion
walkthrough.text: At the very end, leftover resources convert to victory points. Every three Ore, Credits, or Knowledge converts to one point. Each Q.I.C. token is worth one point. Every three power tokens in Bowl three convert to one point. Add these to your total and the player with the most victory points wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 6 rounds of colonizing planets, building structures, researching, forming federations
Highest VP wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Gaia Project. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first two rounds with three example players, each using a different faction. We will cover the key decisions you will face and highlight common beginner mistakes along the way.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Factions
walkthrough.text: Player one is the Terrans, a balanced faction great for learning. Player two is the Hadsch Hallas, who can spend Credits in place of other resources. Player three is the Geodens, who gain Q.I.C. when terraforming. Even if you are playing a different faction, the principles here apply to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Round One Income
walkthrough.text: Let's begin Round one. During the Income Phase, everyone collects their starting income simultaneously. Check the uncovered spaces on your faction board and gather the resources shown. Also collect any income from your round booster. This should give you a modest pool of Ore, Credits, and maybe some Knowledge.

walkthrough.title: Player One Builds a Mine
walkthrough.text: Player one, the Terrans, starts the Action Phase. Let's build a Mine on a nearby Terra planet. Since it matches our native type, we just pay the Ore and Credit cost and place the Mine. Building near our other starting Mine is smart because we will need connected structures later for federations.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Upgrades to a Trading Station
walkthrough.text: Player two, the Hadsch Hallas, upgrades one of their starting Mines to a Trading Station. This unlocks Credit income on their faction board and, because an opponent has a structure nearby, the upgrade costs fewer Credits. That discount is easy to overlook, so always check for adjacent opponents before upgrading.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Researches
walkthrough.text: Player three, the Geodens, spends Knowledge to advance one step on the Terraforming research track. This reduces the Ore cost of future terraforming actions. Research is often the best first action because it makes everything you do afterward cheaper or more powerful.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Spreading Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Player one sees an appealing planet on the far side of the map and builds a Mine there, far from any other structures. This is a classic beginner mistake. Isolated structures cannot contribute to federations, which require a connected cluster with power value of at least seven. Your early Mines should be close enough to eventually link together.

walkthrough.title: Why Clustering Matters
walkthrough.text: Let's do the math. A Planetary Institute is worth four power value, a Trading Station is worth two, and a Mine is worth one. If these three structures are adjacent, that is exactly seven, enough for a federation. But if your Mine is three hexes away, you will need to spend power tokens as satellites to bridge the gap, permanently shrinking your power pool.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Builds Strategically
walkthrough.text: Player two places a new Mine on a planet between their two starting Mines. Now all three structures are within range one of each other. With a future Trading Station upgrade and a Planetary Institute, this cluster will easily reach the seven power value needed for a federation. That is how you plan ahead.

walkthrough.title: Leeching Power in Action
walkthrough.text: When Player two built that Mine near Player three's structure, Player three gets the option to leech power. The new Mine has a power value of one, so Player three can gain one power for free. If the structure had a higher power value, leeching would cost victory points. In the early game, always accept free leeches.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Research Tracks
walkthrough.text: Player one has been building Mines all round but has not advanced on any research track. This is a costly mistake. Research tracks provide technology tiles, income bonuses, and massive end-game victory points. A player at level four on two tracks will outscore a player at level one on all six tracks. Focus your research on two or three tracks that align with your faction and strategy.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Advances to Level Three
walkthrough.text: Player three reaches level three on the Terraforming track and claims a standard technology tile. They choose one that provides Ore income each round. This tile will pay for itself many times over across the remaining five rounds. Reaching level three on any track is a key milestone because of these technology tile rewards.

walkthrough.title: The Power Action Temptation
walkthrough.text: Player one eyes a juicy power action on the research board that would let them terraform a planet for free. But they only have two power in Bowl three and the action costs four. So they burn two power tokens from Bowl two, permanently removing them from the game, to move two tokens into Bowl three. Now they can afford the action.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Burning Too Much Power
walkthrough.text: That burn felt great in the moment, but Player one just permanently shrank their power pool. Burning is tempting because it gives immediate results, but each burned token is power you will never cycle again. In the early rounds, try to limit burns to one or two tokens total. Save aggressive burning for the last two rounds when your engine is fully built.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Booster Selection
walkthrough.text: Player three has run out of productive actions and decides to pass. They return their round booster, collect the pass bonus printed on it, and select a new booster for Round two. Passing early means first pick of boosters, which can be a deliberate strategy. Look at the Round two scoring tile to pick a booster that synergizes with it.

walkthrough.title: Round One Clean Up
walkthrough.text: After all players pass, we resolve the Round one scoring tile. Let's say it awards two victory points for each Mine built this round. Player one built two Mines and earns four points. Player two built one Mine and earns two points. Player three built none and earns zero. Now we advance to Round two.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Begins
walkthrough.text: Round two starts with the Income Phase. Notice how Player two collects more Credits now because their Trading Station uncovered a new income space. This is the engine-building core of Gaia Project. Every structure you build makes your income phase more productive. Let's see what each player prioritizes.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Upgrades to Research Lab
walkthrough.text: Player two upgrades their Trading Station to a Research Lab. This unlocks Knowledge income and lets them immediately claim a standard technology tile. They pick a tile that gives a special action for gaining power. Each upgrade in Gaia Project serves double duty because it both unlocks income and provides an immediate benefit.

walkthrough.title: Player One Corrects Course
walkthrough.text: Player one learned from their Round one mistakes. Instead of building another isolated Mine, they spend Knowledge to advance on the Navigation research track, increasing their building range to two. Now they can reach more planets without needing to build right next to existing structures. They also plan their next Mine to create a tight cluster.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Forms a Federation
walkthrough.text: Player three has been planning carefully. They upgrade a Mine to a Planetary Institute, which is worth four power value. Combined with an adjacent Trading Station at two and a Mine at one, their cluster hits exactly seven. They declare a federation and claim a federation token worth seven victory points.

walkthrough.title: Satellites for Federation
walkthrough.text: Let's say Player two wants to form a federation but their structures are separated by one empty hex. They can place a power token from their supply onto that empty space hex as a satellite. The satellite connects the two groups, and now the combined power value reaches seven. The satellite token is permanently spent, so use this sparingly.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Wrap-Up
walkthrough.text: As Round two ends, take stock of your position. You should have three to five structures on the map, at least one upgrade beyond Mines, a couple of research track advances, and ideally your first federation formed or nearly ready. If you are behind on any of these, adjust your plan for Round three.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the three beginner mistakes we saw. First, spreading structures too thin makes federations impossible without expensive satellites. Second, ignoring research tracks means missing out on technology tiles and end-game points. Third, burning too much power early permanently weakens your engine. Avoid these three traps and you will be competitive.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing this practice tutorial. You have seen how to build Mines, upgrade structures, advance on research tracks, leech power, form federations, and select boosters. The remaining four rounds will follow the same rhythm but with bigger decisions and higher stakes.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals and faction-specific advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick glossary of every term and mechanism in the game.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun colonizing the galaxy!

walkthrough.title: Plan Federations from the Start
walkthrough.text: Federation tokens are one of the biggest sources of victory points in the game. Before you place your very first Mine during setup, think about where your federation clusters will form. A Planetary Institute at four power value is the anchor of most federations. Place your starting Mines within two or three hexes of each other so you can link them with one or two upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Research is King
walkthrough.text: New players often underestimate how powerful the research tracks are. Each level provides escalating bonuses, and reaching level five on any track gives you an advanced technology tile and a huge one-time bonus. Focus on two or three tracks that align with your faction's strengths. A Terraforming-focused faction should push that track hard, while a Knowledge-heavy faction should target Science and Artificial Intelligence.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Round Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: The six round scoring tiles are visible from the very beginning of the game, and aligning your actions with them is essential. If Round two rewards upgrading to Trading Stations, plan your Round one to set up Mines ready for upgrade. If Round four rewards research advances, stockpile Knowledge in Round three. The best players plan two rounds ahead, not just the current one.

walkthrough.title: Leech Power Aggressively
walkthrough.text: When an opponent builds near your structures and you get the chance to leech power, take it in most situations. The victory point cost seems expensive at first glance, but power in Bowl three is incredibly flexible. You can use it for power actions, to fund Gaiaforming, or to place satellites for federations. The only time to decline is when you are already flush with power and the VP cost is steep.

walkthrough.title: Use Your Faction Ability Early
walkthrough.text: Every faction has a unique ability, and the best players figure out how to exploit theirs from the very first round. Hadsch Hallas should leverage their Credit flexibility to grab expensive upgrades before opponents can. Geodens should terraform aggressively to collect Q.I.C. tokens. Nevlas should abuse their ability to use power without spending it. Read your faction sheet carefully and build your entire strategy around what makes your faction special.

walkthrough.title: Build Around the Final Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: The two final scoring tiles are worth up to thirty-six victory points combined, making them the single largest scoring opportunity in the game. If a tile rewards structures in federations, prioritize forming multiple federations. If it rewards planet type diversity, colonize as many different planet types as possible. Identify which final scoring tile aligns best with your faction and push hard toward it all game.

walkthrough.title: Booster Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: Round boosters are selected when you pass, and passing early gives you first pick. In the late game, some boosters are dramatically more valuable depending on your board state. The boosters that award victory points based on your structures or research levels become huge in Rounds five and six. Do not underestimate the power of a well-timed early pass to lock in the perfect booster.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes Recap
walkthrough.text: Let's summarize the biggest beginner traps. First, placing starting Mines too far apart makes federations nearly impossible. Second, ignoring research tracks means missing powerful technology tiles and end-game points. Third, burning too much power early permanently cripples your engine. Fourth, not reading all six round scoring tiles at the start of the game means you cannot plan ahead. Avoid these four mistakes and you will compete in any game of Gaia Project.

summary.title: Plan Federations from the Start
bullets: Place starting Mines within 2-3 hexes of each other
PI (4) + Trading Station (2) + Mine (1) = 7 for federation
Isolated structures need expensive satellites to connect

summary.title: Research is King
bullets: Focus on 2-3 tracks aligned with your faction
Level 5 = advanced tech tile + huge one-time bonus
Each level provides escalating passive bonuses

summary.title: Watch the Round Scoring Tiles
bullets: All 6 tiles visible from game start — plan 2 rounds ahead
Align actions with current round's scoring tile
Set up Mines in Round N to upgrade for Round N+1 scoring

summary.title: Leech Power Aggressively
bullets: VP cost usually worth it for Bowl III flexibility
Power fuels actions, Gaiaforming, and satellites
Decline only when already flush with power

summary.title: Use Your Faction Ability Early
bullets: Build entire strategy around your unique ability
Hadsch Hallas: Credit flexibility for early upgrades
Geodens: terraform often for Q.I.C. income
Nevlas: use power without spending it

summary.title: Build Around the Final Scoring Tiles
bullets: Up to 36 VP combined — largest scoring opportunity
Identify which tile aligns with your faction
Push toward it from Round 1 through Round 6

summary.title: Booster Selection Strategy
bullets: Passing early = first pick of boosters
Late-game boosters with VP bonuses become huge in Rounds 5-6
Match booster to next round's scoring tile

summary.title: Common Mistakes Recap
bullets: Starting Mines too far apart → can't form federations
Ignoring research → miss tech tiles and end-game VP
Burning too much power early → permanently crippled engine
Not reading all 6 scoring tiles at start → can't plan ahead

walkthrough.title: Research Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest openings is racing to level five on a research track before anyone else claims the advanced technology tile. Identify which track aligns with your faction and push it from Round one. Geodens should rush Terraforming, Nevlas should rush Economy or Science, and Terrans can target whichever track is least contested. The advanced tiles are first-come-first-served, so speed is everything.

walkthrough.title: Federation Chaining
walkthrough.text: Expert players do not just form one federation and move on. They plan their structure placement to enable multiple federations over the course of the game. The key insight is that once structures are locked into a federation, they cannot count again. So build new structures adjacent to your existing federation and upgrade them to create a second cluster that meets the seven power value threshold independently.

walkthrough.title: Navigation versus Terraforming
walkthrough.text: These two tracks represent a fundamental strategic choice. Investing in Navigation lets you reach distant planets without terraforming, expanding your options across the galaxy. Investing in Terraforming lets you colonize any nearby planet cheaply, even those far from your native type on the wheel. Your choice depends on the map layout. If your native planets are scattered, prioritize Navigation. If they are clustered but surrounded by non-native types, prioritize Terraforming.

walkthrough.title: Power Economy Management
walkthrough.text: Managing your power bowls is the most nuanced skill in Gaia Project. In the early game, focus on cycling power naturally through leeching and income rather than burning. By Round three or four, identify whether you need burst power for a critical action or steady cycling for multiple small actions. Burning two to three tokens in the mid-game is acceptable if it enables a federation or a crucial power action that scores immediate points.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Denial and Map Control
walkthrough.text: At higher player counts, map awareness becomes critical. If an opponent needs a specific planet to complete their federation cluster, colonizing it first or nearby forces them to spend extra resources. Watch what planets your opponents need and consider whether denying them is worth your action. The best denial plays double as expansion for your own strategy rather than pure spite moves.

walkthrough.title: Passing Tempo
walkthrough.text: The order in which players pass shapes the entire next round. Passing first gives you the best booster selection but means fewer actions this round. In Rounds one through three, taking more actions is usually worth more than booster priority. But in Rounds four through six, the pass bonuses on boosters can be worth ten or more victory points. Learn to recognize when an early pass nets you more points from a booster than another action would.

walkthrough.title: Satellite Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Satellites connect structures for federations but cost power tokens permanently. Minimizing satellite usage is an advanced skill. The ideal federation uses zero satellites, with all structures naturally adjacent. When satellites are necessary, place them where they connect the maximum number of structures. Never use three satellites when repositioning a Mine to a closer planet would only cost two.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final two rounds, shift your mindset from engine building to point extraction. Every action should directly generate victory points through round scoring tiles, federation formation, or research advances. Convert leftover resources into structures or research levels rather than hoarding them. Remember that every three leftover resources only convert to one point, so it is almost always better to spend them on an action worth two or more points.

summary.title: Research Rush Strategy
bullets: Race to level 5 on a track for the advanced tech tile
Advanced tiles are first-come-first-served — speed is key
Pick the track that aligns with your faction's strengths

summary.title: Federation Chaining
bullets: Plan for multiple federations, not just one
Structures in a federation can't count again
Build new clusters adjacent to existing federations

summary.title: Navigation versus Terraforming
bullets: Navigation: reach distant native planets without terraform cost
Terraforming: colonize nearby non-native planets cheaply
Choose based on map layout and native planet distribution

summary.title: Power Economy Management
bullets: Early game: cycle power naturally through leeching and income
Mid-game: burn 2-3 tokens if it enables a federation or key action
Late game: power pool should sustain itself without burns

summary.title: Opponent Denial and Map Control
bullets: Colonize planets opponents need for their federations
Best denial plays also advance your own strategy
Map awareness is critical at 3-4 players

summary.title: Passing Tempo
bullets: Early pass = best booster pick but fewer actions
Rounds 1-3: more actions usually better than booster priority
Rounds 4-6: pass bonuses on boosters can exceed 10 VP

summary.title: Satellite Efficiency
bullets: Satellites cost power tokens permanently
Ideal federation uses 0 satellites — plan tight clusters
When needed, connect maximum structures per satellite

summary.title: End-Game Point Maximization
bullets: Rounds 5-6: shift from engine building to point extraction
Every action should directly generate VP
Spend resources on actions rather than hoarding (3 resources = 1 VP)

entries.definition: The most basic structure. Costs Ore and Credits to build on a colonized planet. Generates Ore income. Power value of 1 for federations.
Upgraded from a Mine. Generates Credit income. Power value of 2. Building cost is reduced if adjacent to an opponent's structure.
Upgraded from a Trading Station. Generates Knowledge income and grants a standard technology tile when built.
Upgraded from a Trading Station. Unlocks your faction's special ability and generates Power income. Power value of 4, making it the anchor of most federations. Each faction has exactly one.
Upgraded from a Research Lab. Grants an advanced technology tile when built. Generates Knowledge income. Each faction has two Academies but can only build one per upgrade path.

A special token placed on Transdimensional planets to begin the Gaiaforming process. Requires committing power to the Gaia area. After the Gaia Phase, the planet becomes a Gaia planet and the Gaiaformer returns to you.
A power token placed on an empty space hex to connect structures for federation purposes. Permanently spent from your power pool. Used to bridge gaps between non-adjacent structures.
A basic resource generated primarily by Mines. Used to build structures, terraform planets, and pay upgrade costs. Brown token.
A basic resource generated primarily by Trading Stations. Used alongside Ore for most building and upgrade costs. Gold token.
A resource generated primarily by Research Labs and Academies. Spent to advance on research tracks. Green token.

Quantum Intelligence Cubes. A special resource earned through the Artificial Intelligence track and certain abilities. Spent on powerful Q.I.C. actions: 1 for a free tech tile, 2 for VP per planet type, 4 for a Mine ignoring range.
Tokens that cycle through three bowls on your faction board. Gained through income, leeching, and technology tiles. Spent from Bowl III for power actions. Spent tokens return to Bowl I.
The first area in the power cycle. Tokens start here or return here after being spent from Bowl III. When you gain power, tokens move from Bowl I to Bowl II first.

The second area in the power cycle. Tokens move here from Bowl I when gaining power, then to Bowl III. You may burn tokens from Bowl II, permanently removing them, to move an equal number from Bowl II to Bowl III.
The third area in the power cycle. Only tokens here can be spent on power actions. Spent tokens return to Bowl I. This is your spendable power reserve.
A fourth power area used exclusively for Gaiaforming. Power tokens committed here during Gaiaforming return to Bowl I during the next Gaia Phase.
Permanently remove power tokens from Bowl II to move an equal number of tokens from Bowl II to Bowl III. Provides immediate spendable power but permanently shrinks your power pool.

When an opponent builds or upgrades within range 2 of your structure, you may gain power equal to the new structure's power value. Costs 1 VP per power gained beyond the first. You may decline or take fewer than the maximum.

A connected group of structures with a combined power value of at least 7. Forming a federation lets you claim a federation token worth VP and resources. Structures in a federation cannot count toward another federation.
Claimed when forming a federation. Each token provides a combination of VP, resources, and power. Tokens are limited by type. Some provide one-time bonuses, others ongoing benefits.

Each structure type has a power value used for federation calculations and leeching. Mine = 1, Trading Station = 2, Research Lab = 3 (for leeching) or 2 (some versions), Planetary Institute = 4, Academy = 5 (for leeching) or 3.

The process of converting a non-native planet to your faction's native type so you can build on it. Costs Ore per step on the transformation wheel. The Terraforming research track reduces the Ore cost per step.
A circular diagram showing the seven planet types arranged so each type is a certain number of steps from every other. Used to calculate terraforming costs between planet types.
The maximum distance in hexes between your existing structure and a planet you want to colonize. Starts at 1, increased by the Navigation research track. At Navigation level 5, range is unlimited.

A one-time action paid for by spending power tokens from Bowl III. Power actions are printed on the research board and include gaining resources, terraforming, or placing structures. Each power action can only be taken once per round across all players.

A powerful one-time action paid for with Q.I.C. tokens. Three options: spend 1 Q.I.C. for a free tech tile, 2 Q.I.C. for 3 VP per planet of a chosen type, or 4 Q.I.C. to place a Mine anywhere ignoring range.
An action provided by a technology tile, round booster, or faction ability. Marked with a unique icon. Each special action can only be used once per round and resets at the start of the next round.
The process of converting a Transdimensional planet into a Gaia planet. Requires placing a Gaiaformer and committing power to the Gaia area. Resolves during the next round's Gaia Phase.

One of six advancement tracks on the research board. Advancing costs Knowledge. Each track provides escalating bonuses. Level 3 grants a standard tech tile, level 5 grants an advanced tech tile and a large bonus.
Research track that reduces the Ore cost per terraforming step. At level 0, each step costs 3 Ore. At level 5, you can terraform any planet in a single step regardless of distance on the wheel.
Research track that increases your building range. Starts at range 1. At level 5, your range is unlimited, letting you colonize any unoccupied planet on the map.
Research track that provides Q.I.C. income. Higher levels grant more Q.I.C. per round. At level 5, you receive 4 Q.I.C. per round, fueling powerful Q.I.C. actions.
Research track that reduces the power cost to start Gaiaforming projects. At level 5, Gaiaforming is free, requiring no power commitment to the Gaia area.
Research track that provides Credit and Ore income at higher levels. A strong general-purpose track that fuels building and upgrading without needing specific planet access.
Research track that provides Knowledge income at higher levels. Useful for factions that want to advance rapidly on multiple research tracks simultaneously.
A technology tile gained at research level 3-4 or when upgrading to a Research Lab. Provides ongoing income, one-time bonuses, or a special action. Multiple copies available.

A powerful technology tile gained at research level 5 or when upgrading to an Academy. Only one per research track, claimed by the first player to reach level 5. Provides large VP, abilities, or resources.

One tile placed per round during setup. Awards VP for specific actions taken during that round, such as building Mines, upgrading structures, or advancing on research tracks. All 6 tiles are visible from game start.

A tile selected during the pass action that provides income each round and a pass bonus when returned. Player count + 3 boosters are available each game. Selecting a strong booster can be worth passing early.

VP printed on a round booster that you collect when you pass and return the booster. Typically scales with your board state, such as VP per Research Lab or VP per Mine. Increasingly valuable in later rounds.

Two tiles selected during setup that determine end-game bonus VP categories. Awards 18/12/6/0 VP for 1st through 4th place. Categories include structures in federations, planet types colonized, structures on map, sectors with presence, and research advancement.
One of seven planet types. Native to Hadsch Hallas and Bescods. Yellow/tan colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Taklons and Ambas. Dark green colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Geodens and Bal T'aks. Red/orange colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Firaks and a second faction. Dark red/black colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Lantids and Ivits. Gray/silver colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Nevlas and Itars. White/light blue colored on the map.
One of seven planet types. Native to Terrans and Xenos. Blue/Earth-like colored on the map.
A special purple/gray planet type that cannot be directly terraformed. Must be Gaiaformed first by placing a Gaiaformer and committing power. After the Gaia Phase it becomes a Gaia planet.
A green planet created when a Transdimensional planet is Gaiaformed. All factions treat Gaia planets as just one terraforming step from their native type, making them relatively cheap to colonize.
One of 10 double-sided modular tiles that form the galaxy map. Each tile shows a mix of planet types and empty space hexes. The modular layout ensures every game has a unique map.
A beginner-friendly faction native to Terra planets. Balanced abilities with no extreme strengths or weaknesses. Good for learning the game's core mechanics.
A faction native to Desert planets. Can spend Credits as any other resource, making them an economic powerhouse with incredible flexibility in resource management.
A faction native to Oxide planets. Gain Q.I.C. tokens when terraforming, rewarding aggressive expansion across different planet types.
A faction native to Ice planets. Can use power from Bowl III without actually spending it, giving them an enormous power action economy.
A faction native to Titanium planets. Start with no Mines and instead place Space Stations. Can create a Lost Planet hex and form federations differently from other factions.
A faction native to Ice planets. Can convert burned power tokens into Gaiaformers, turning the normally permanent cost of burning power into a unique advantage.
The first phase of each round where all players simultaneously collect resources from their uncovered faction board tracks and round booster.
The second phase of each round where active Gaiaforming projects resolve. Power tokens in the Gaia area return to Bowl I, and Transdimensional planets become Gaia planets.
The third and main phase of each round. Players alternate taking one action each clockwise until all have passed. Seven action types are available.
